transcript.text: Hi, Tyler.
Doing well.
How are you?
This meeting is being recorded.
Yeah, I'm doing pretty well.
What part of London are you calling in from?
I'm at our office in Soho.
Are you basically in Houston?
No, I'm in Houston, but I was in London not too long ago.
Had a good buddy who got married in Lisbon and, you know, you're traveling that far.
got to make a trip out of it.
What part of London do you live in?
I live in Islington, which is like north-central London.
It's been good.
I've been here for two years.
As you can guess by my voice, I'm not British.
I'm from the U.S.
originally.
But I moved out here end of 2023 with my fiancé.
We thought it would be a fun adventure.
And so far, it's been great.
No complaints.
Happy we did the move.
Very cool.
Yeah, one of my friends, who I went to Texas A&M with, he lives over in Kensington.
And they're loving it as well and definitely got a lot of good recommendations along the way.
Even checked out Shakespeare's Globe.
I don't know if you've ever been there before.
Yeah, I have.
I haven't seen a show, but I've been over there.
Did you watch the show?
Yeah, they had the Crucible playing, which was like the first non-Shakespeare production that was at the Globe, I think.
And, man, like that, you know, it takes place back in like the Salem witchcraft era and just the whole environment and aesthetic transported you a little bit for sure.
That's cool.
I haven't thought about the Crucible since like seventh grade when I had to write a book report on it.
I don't think I appreciated it very much then, but I'm sure I would enjoy it now.
It's so funny.
I feel like every American student cross paths with that story like earlier than they should have.
Should have been like a high school project.
Yeah, exactly.
Nice.
Well, I saw you've worked at some relevant companies to Eleven Labs.
You were pretty early at AIR.
I don't know if AIR exists anymore.
Does it?
Yeah, they're still kicking.
And I think just the marketing engine that they've built, you know, they're doing stuff with like Kareem Rama and have gotten a lot of love from the creative community.
So, yeah, they're still doing pretty well.
Oh, maybe I was thinking of a different company then.
I was, there's an AIR that's doing like voice agents, which is why I thought of it, but.
Okay.
No, AIR is like a creative operation slash digital asset management tool.
Oh, very cool.
Nice.
Well, have you heard of Eleven Labs before?
I was getting myself familiar before the call and really cool stuff that you guys are working on.
And we've created some content for Vappy, who I'm sure.
Are they like a direct competitor, would you say?
They're both, I would say, frenemies.
We love them because they're a customer of ours.
But we also offer a product that's competitive as well.
Okay, gotcha.
But yeah, I mean, I kind of got the high level of what you guys do.
But, you know, maybe you could give me like a quick intro of what's the most relevant.
And also would love to learn how you came across GrowthX.
So, Elevenlabs started as a foundational research company for AI audio.
So you could think of us like an anthropic or open AI, but specifically building foundational models across text-to-speech, voice cloning, dubbing, sound effects, and now music.
The company went super viral in 2023 because...
went went is
Peter, our CTO, basically made the first text-to-speech that actually sounded like a person.
And the initial product was just a text box on the internet.
You input text and you get audio out.
And it blew up right away.
And then we've since then built different tools on top of that and also made the models available via API.
And so we largely now have two or three product offerings, depending on how you look at it.
There's a creative platform that's for content creators to make voiceovers for YouTube, podcasts, learning and development videos, all of that.
And we sell that from everyone, from a YouTuber up to marketing teams at Fortune 500.
We have our APIs, which are all of our foundational models that we make available to other companies.
VAPI being one of those who embed.

Our text-to-speech in their voice agent platform, but it could also be like we do, we just did a partnership with Chess.com where they clone the voice of Magnus Carlsen, and then you can learn Chess while talking to Magnus.
And then we offer agents platform, which is the reason I said we're in some ways competitive to Vapi.
Our focus there is more on the enterprise.
But we sell voice agents that you can embed in your business for customer support, for lead gen, for learning and development role play.

And we'll sell that along with sometimes selling forward-deployed engineering services to large enterprises, and then we'll go in and build both voice but also text-based agents that they can use for customer engagement.
The The reason
The I'm reaching out now, I mean, we're, I'm sure you see this with a lot of the companies you work with, but hyper growth company doing a couple hundred million in ARR now.
We service millions of customers on the B2C side and then thousands of great companies on the enterprise side.

We only have 300 people internally, which feels like a lot compared to where we were when, when I joined, but still there's just like a lot more that we want to do that we can't with the resources we have.
And what initially had me reach out, one of my colleagues sent me a link and he said, let me see what he said specifically.
Yeah, this is, this is great, Sam.
Thanks for all the context and yeah, super, super cool what you guys are doing.
There's like so many applications and I'm sure sometimes you think about, you know, who's going to know what's real and what's not.
comes to who they're hearing, right?
But, you know, can't let that get in the way of making money, driving, The referral I got, one of my colleagues met with someone who just started a new marketing job, and she recommended us.
I don't have her name or her company, unfortunately, but she mentioned that you all were great for content strategy and used by Lovable and Ramp.
And in particular, I just have an endless need for thought leadership content because, like, it performs very well on LinkedIn, both organic and as a gated document ad for LinkedIn.
My sales team wants to use it in outreach, and in general, we're trying to position ourselves as the thought leaders in this voice agent space, and there's a lot of verticals we sell to.
And we just need more content.
Prove that we're experts.
And in a lot of cases, we have the insights.
We just don't have the time or space to generate all the content we want to.
That makes a ton of sense.
When you talk about like having the insights, where do those live?
And I guess how well documented is some of that information?
The reason I ask, a lot of times when we start off with a client, we'll do more of the foundational like SEO content that will live on their blog.
We'll be less thought leadership, more just optimizing for search.
And from there, we typically move to more thought leadership style content because we have to train, right?
Like our engine and provide the context necessary to make it more thought leadership and opinion based as opposed to just the most well-researched, right?
Like educational, informational intent.
content type search content.
And then we'll also look at like programmatic use cases.
So one thing that we did with VAPI and also RAMP, with VAPI, we created a bunch of these different voice agent pages for them.
And, you know, you can preview the assistant, it's got this config that we've built in information around like the challenge and solution respective to that use case.
And then, you know, it goes on and on, right?
Like, electronic health records, and there's like hundreds of these pages.

And then for RAMP, what we did is we used their internal spend data to create this vendor directory, where we populated all of these pages with, you know, content around how much businesses are spending for the most popular tools.
And also like alternatives pages versus pages.
So now, like, if you search Airtable versus ClickUp, you start to see RAMP sided with
Content we've created.
Way more context than I thought I'd give there.
But yeah, guess, you know, do you have much of a need on like the pure editorial SEO content?
Or do you feel like you guys have that well under control?
Like you obviously have great domain authority and traction.
So just getting a sense of like where we could start in a partnership like this.
Yeah, well, I mean, we always want to do more for SEO.
It's not directly my focus area, though, which is why I'm not obsessed with it as a problem statement.
We I'm definitely can send your info over to our SEO lead.
He's generating programmatic pages today for the agents thing you just shared.
Like he just produced today 30 pages of different AI agents answering service for hospital answering service for dentist office.
So I
I think that kind of stuff is not so much the bottleneck for us.
It's like the one we just did that I think is great is how to think about choosing the best voice for your enterprise.

And it's like quite researched around some insights around how different regions or countries or people react to different accents and voices, businesses we worked with to choose and craft the perfect voice, the prompt we use to generate their voice, reactions from customers.
It's like a voice sommelier type piece.
The way that that information came together was largely interviews of people within the company.
And customers.
So it was not done.
I would not say the info was well documented before.
And some of the other pieces that we've done, it often starts with like a Slack thread where there's two engineers who are talking or debating something.
We like copy down all of the content and then use that as the, and then we'll go interview them more and generate the content around it.
Okay.
Gotcha.
Yeah.

Cause to pull back the curtain on like the way we work and how we actually create content, what we do is we have an eight week strategy sprint and then a 12 month growth execution where we're ideally like trying to publish daily and we'll do the staging and publishing.
We'll do all the LLM visibility and monitoring.
And so most clients see us as an extension of their content team.
But if you take like a really simple example here.
We're, you know, Engine is in the business travel space.
We created all these different business travel guides on their site.
And the first thing that we're doing is we're generating these artifacts that give the context engineering to the workflows.
And so for Engine, like the company context, the products and services, but also they focus on different industries.
So we created like these industry personas artifacts.
And with GEO, it's all about, you know, can you get in the mind of your ICP and then create content that has a large enough surface area across all of the ways that they may go in and prompt.
And so with your use case, like we would be creating artifacts that would be more around how we could create thought leadership content, which would be a bit more custom, right?
We might set up pipelines that are different than like our agentic, you know, editorial.

Workflows that we leverage, but I think the key here is we would, you know, I know, I know our CEO Marcel would be very interested in this use case because of who your brand is and his experience, like with the Vappy use case and even at, you know, Deepgram, it's like similar types of products and services.
I think it would just be more custom in nature.
So I can dig in anywhere that you'd like, but would love to just kind of get your thoughts.
Yeah, so, well, yeah, I guess I'm wondering, so the product is cool, by the way.
So basically you, the first, this initial eight week setup, you're talking about these artifacts.
This is basically like download of all of the context you can get about the business.
And then once we have this, we're generating net new pieces.
this, and...
And...

This is kind of like a super complicated system prompt and knowledge base that is, we then, when we want to generate a net new piece, we might give it two to three insights, say we want to talk about X.
It's taking all of this into account and then we'll generate a net new piece for the blog.
Is that the idea of how this works?
Exactly.
And to show you like a tactical example here, you know, augment code, and this is probably a good one to look at in terms of thought leadership that we could create at scale very quickly.
That would be maybe less custom.
Like we've done everything on their guides and they want to own the space for enterprise AI coding tools.
And so they're up against cursor, they're up against GitHub.
And any piece of content that you see here, and some of it is tool comparisons, you know, some of it is more.
Or just recommendations within the space.
But if you take like one assignment that we created for Augmented Code, all of these inputs, we've got an input from like the managing editor.
We've got these artifacts around their personas and their company.
We're using like either a keyword or a article idea.
And then what's happening in the background to generate this brief is we're going in and looking at organic results, keyword traffic, SERP ranking factors, competitor content, search intent.

And so with our workflows, like we set up a chain of thought where we're breaking down these individual steps and then just building on the outputs with more context, with more inputs, leveraging different models for different use cases.
And so this is just to generate the brief for an assignment.
a you can can can can can you can
When it comes to actually using this brief to create a piece of content, that's where things get a lot more involved, and I can show you what that looks like as well.

But yes, to your point, it would be dependent on the inputs that we can provide, which we can ingest resources from your team for, but we also set up a kickoff call, a product deep dive, and that is also very informative to capture some of the nuance.
Yeah, I'm thinking, I mean, this would be useful.

Largely, though, what I'm trying to solve for would be like, and I don't think we have cracked this yet, I just want one piece of content that's so good that a telco, like, putting a specific example, it'd be like the state of voice agents for the telecommunications industry.
And I want one white paper so good that someone would read it and send it.
To a friend and be like, oh, you should read this.
It's very informative.
So I don't need like to publish one a day.
I'm not even expecting to.
I want to publish like one a month, maybe even once a quarter.
And of course, you know, scale up from there.
But we've yet to crack the code where we've even feel like we've nailed just, you know, some of these foundational pieces.
But it doesn't sound like that's exactly what your sweet spot is.
It's not what we've traditionally done.
But I know for like the right logos, we'll think outside of the box.
And for use cases that push our team forward, either from a product or a service delivery POV, that can be really appealing.
So I think, let me talk to Marcel directly about this.
Um, you mentioned, like, which.
You talked about like the thought leadership.
I feel like I have a good grasp of that.
Talk to me more about like the case studies and like where, like, would you need us to do the design and the web development for gated content or would we work with someone on your team for that?
Well, the gated content and thought leadership is kind of similar.
I'm kind of thinking of them in one in the same, but we, for our gated content, we'll just repurpose the same piece potentially a couple of different times.
So like, we might take, we'll write one piece on, on the voice, um, best voice for your business.
And then we'll package that up for India and add a couple of India specific insights and then might do it for a couple of different regions.
Um, or more tactically though, like would someone on your team create the page with the form fill or would you be expecting?
I'm trying to get a sense of if we're just words or if we're also like creating new page templates for you.
No, we have the pages and the design piece.
We have a template and our design team does it, although I'm sure they'd be very happy if we could outsource the production of like the actual PDF from the words.
Okay, perfect.
And case studies, yeah, what's the scale that you were thinking of with case studies and how much of that do you have?
It doesn't have to be like well organized, but do you have all the context that we would leverage to create the case studies?
Yeah, the case studies piece is a bit, well, this was the one that I really tried to automate with AI.
The current status quo today is there's kind of two tiers of case study.
We have the A-tier companies that are the best logos in the world.
That we really want to do case studies with, and they have 100 person comms teams that make it very hard for me to get approvals.
And so that's like very high touch, back and forth, negotiating with them to get these done.
And then I'm guessing there's a consumer play where you could create a ton of case studies.

Well, yeah, then there's actually even on our enterprise side, like every single startup or mid-market company that signs up for us will ask us to do a case study, which is never a position I've been in before.
But they're like, we would love you to write about our use case and publish it on the blog.
And there's, I mean, it's getting to the point where it's like 20 a week.
And so what we've done, we will send them a questionnaire, and we say, fill out this questionnaire.
They say,
Send me the responses.
I put it into ChatGPT along with a couple of sample case studies, and then we generate the output.
And usually we'll just take that and put it on the blog.

So it's not dissimilar to what you've described, but our system prompt and the context we're providing could probably be a lot better because it's not nearly as sophisticated as what you're showing there.
Understood.
And how would us creating those case studies benefit the business?
Is that just increasing how fast you can acquire SMB and mid-market customers?
It's a very clear connection to ROI with the thought leadership use case, but how do these case studies benefit you guys?
Yeah, well, one, it's just nice to have long tail of more case studies because there's we sell to so many different industries and different.
countries.
if someone comes and asks me, do we have a FinServe case study in Korea?
You know, I get it.
Yeah.
Sales always benefits from case studies.
And, you know, it's like if you're selling to so many different types of customers to have a case study for every single scenario is huge, huge leg up.
It removes friction from the sales cycle, immediately builds confidence with the prospect.
Okay.
Interesting.
Yeah.
I apologize for not having a ton of context for you today, but I'd love to like share resources, talk to Marcel about the specifics here.
And if he's interested, I think in the next call, we can do a much deeper dive into how tactically like we could execute.
It's just different than our standard engagement, which is like client comes in, they need to scale quality.
And we are the partner that's like creating all of their editorial content and even programmatic.

In some scenarios, I think if you want to just send, you know, maybe forward my email to the SEO counterpart, see if there's any need around some of our bread and butter, and then we can come into this next conversation either just with you or with you and the SEO side.
I'll bring Marcel, and then we'll dig more into like how we could execute.
Sounds great.
Yeah, and one thing I just want to add, what I'm asking for is difficult and like on the realm of unreasonable.
So, and what I mean by that specifically is like the fact that we haven't internally cracked what we think is a amazing thought leadership piece.
one, like the bar of what we're trying to achieve is really high, but I also think, you know, what we're asking for someone externally to do, it'll be even harder.

So that's why I'm looking for someone who, I'm looking for someone who's like, you know, they both have the writing expertise, but also can help steer us in the direction of here, if you want to produce pieces that are truly great, here are the insights that you need to get.
And we might not share those initially, and you might come back and say, this is not enough information to actually do this.
If you wanted this to be great, you would need X.
Then we'd scramble and try to find that data or that information and kind of go until we get there.

But we just haven't, we really haven't hit the part where we feel like we're publishing something where we're like, you know, this is something that everyone's going to share internally in Slack, because the insights are just that amazing.
Makes a lot of sense.
And just to confirm, it would be like...
One bigger thought leadership piece per month is around the scope we'd be looking at, and then about 20 case studies per month, would you say, would be the ideal deliverables?
Yeah.
Okay.
I mean, really, the one thought leadership piece is the number one thing I'm angling for.
The case studies, if we could do that together, great.
But, I mean, I think I have a decent solution for that now.
But the one thought leadership piece is the biggest gap for us.

Yeah, I think what, just like from talking to Marcel a lot, I think what would be a perf, or maybe not perfect, but what would be a really good fit is if we could start off on editorial, maybe refreshing existing content, creating net new where there's gaps, if we could do that in tandem with the one thought leadership piece per month, time that do, what but
will allow us to work on something that will directly benefit more of the custom use case.
And I think if there's some interest there, then let's definitely set up the follow-up deep dive.
And in the meantime, you definitely clearly articulated what you guys are looking for.
So I'll just have a direct conversation with our CEO and figure out if that's something in our wheelhouse right now or if it's something we need to look at in the future.
Sounds great.
All right.
thank you for the time, Tyler.
appreciate it.
Yeah, great to meet you.
And thanks for all the context on Eleven Labs.
I should have known more.
You guys are killing it.
Yeah, you too.
See you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Intro call to explore a content partnership between ElevenLabs and GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=198.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Primary Need:** ElevenLabs' top priority is one "amazing" thought leadership piece per month—a single, shareable whitepaper that establishes them as the definitive expert in the voice agent space.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1051.0)
  - [**Secondary Need:** Automate the production of \~20 SMB/mid-market case studies per week to meet high demand and provide sales with relevant assets.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1224.0)
  - [**Proposed Path:** GrowthX may offer a custom engagement for the thought leadership, but prefers a hybrid model combining that with its standard SEO content services to build foundational knowledge.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1593.0)
  - [**Next Step:** Tyler will discuss the custom request with GrowthX CEO Marcel; a follow-up call will include Marcel and ElevenLabs' SEO lead to explore the hybrid model.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1408.0)

## Topics

### ElevenLabs Context & Needs

  - [**Company:** AI audio foundational model company (text-to-speech, voice cloning, etc.) with \~$200M ARR and 300 employees.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)
  - [**Products:**](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=266.0)
      - [**Creative Platform:** B2C/B2B tool for content creators.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=266.0)
      - [**APIs:** Models for other companies to embed (e.g., VAPI, Chess.com).](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=289.0)
      - [**Agents Platform:** Enterprise voice agents for customer support, lead gen, and L\&D.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=318.0)
  - [**Primary Need: Thought Leadership**](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=448.0)
      - [**Goal:** Establish ElevenLabs as the definitive expert in the voice agent space.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=448.0)
      - [**Challenge:** Lacks the internal bandwidth to produce high-quality, shareable content despite having the insights.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=448.0)
      - [**Example:** A whitepaper on "The state of voice agents for the telecommunications industry" that is so good it gets shared internally.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1051.0)
  - [**Secondary Need: Case Studies**](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1224.0)
      - [**Challenge:** High demand from SMB/mid-market customers (20+/week) for case studies on their use cases.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1282.0)
      - [**Current Process:** Inefficient; uses a simple questionnaire and ChatGPT, yielding inconsistent quality.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1282.0)
      - [**Goal:** Automate production to provide sales with a long-tail library of use cases across industries and regions.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1349.0)

### GrowthX Proposed Solution

  - [**Standard Model:** An 8-week strategy sprint to build a knowledge base ("artifacts"), followed by 12 months of daily, agentic content production (SEO, programmatic).](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=751.0)
  - [**Proposed Hybrid Engagement:**](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1593.0)
      - [**Custom Thought Leadership:** A custom workflow for the high-quality whitepapers.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1114.0)
      - [**Standard SEO Content:** Run in parallel to build foundational knowledge and train the system, which directly benefits the custom thought leadership work.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1593.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tyler:** Discuss the custom thought leadership request with GrowthX CEO Marcel.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1114.0)
  - [**Sam:** Forward Tyler's contact info to the ElevenLabs SEO lead to explore a potential SEO content partnership.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1440.0)
  - [**Both:** Schedule a follow-up call with Tyler, Marcel, Sam, and the ElevenLabs SEO lead to discuss the hybrid engagement model.](https://fathom.video/share/Np5qavNqXHwxcxGVw3kobipeu8zZbyyV?tab=summary&timestamp=1440.0)

action_items.description: Email SEO lead re: GrowthX; fwd Tyler's contact
Sync w/ Marcel re: Eleven Labs thought leadership/case studies; if fit, schedule deep-dive w/ Sam + Marcel
